title: Higher-for-Longer Entrenched as Warsh-Led Fed Turns Hawkish on Sticky Inflation
content: A hawkish Fed regime under new chair Kevin Warsh dominated cross-asset pricing through the window. At his first meeting the Fed held at 3.50-3.75% but stripped its 2026 cut, issued a notably terser statement shorn of forward guidance, and saw roughly half of officials pencil in hikes. Headline inflation stayed above target while core proved stickier than hoped, prompting BofA to forecast three 2026 hikes toward 4.25-4.5% and to call price pressures unambiguously worse. The repricing kept long-end USTs heavy, the 30-year having printed multi-year highs above 5% and the 10-year holding over 4%, while the USD stayed firm. Higher-for-longer pressured rate-sensitive and long-duration assets, reinforcing the rotation in equity leadership and leaving rates and FX hostage to each inflation print.

title: Great Rotation Out of Mega-Cap AI Into Value, Cyclicals and Small-Caps
content: Equity leadership broadened decisively as AI capex fatigue eroded the mega-cap tech bid that had powered prior windows. Investors demanded clearer returns on vast AI infrastructure spend, and technology and communication services lagged amid cost and demand worries, with Alphabet sliding around 5% on AI talent departures and chipmakers selling off. Capital rotated toward value, cyclicals and defensives, with industrials such as Caterpillar and Deere up roughly 6% alongside transports, energy, staples, utilities and healthcare, while small-caps outperformed large-caps. Market breadth improved markedly, with the share of S&P 500 names above their 50-day average climbing back above 60% from around half at the month's start. Although the S&P slipped modestly in June, the broadening was read as a healthier, less concentrated regime as higher rates challenged rich growth multiples.

title: Iran-Hormuz Oil Risk Premium Unwinds but Fragile Talks Keep Energy Volatile
content: The Middle East energy premium that had spiked on the Strait of Hormuz crisis unwound over the window, easing a key inflation swing factor. Shipping slowly resumed under a US-Iran memorandum of understanding, allowing Brent to slide from its conflict highs toward the mid-to-high $70s and briefly lower, which softened headline inflation fears and supported the broader tape. The de-escalation proved fragile: US-Iran peace talks in Switzerland were abruptly postponed, nudging crude back up, while disputes over Hormuz transit fees and maritime administration left a residual tail risk. The whiplash kept oil, FX and energy equities headline-driven and fed directly into the rates narrative through inflation expectations, with falling crude helping cap long-end yields even as unresolved geopolitics prevented the risk premium from disappearing entirely.
